turns.content: I think I can just use tokio::sync::Barrier and pass in the number of tasks, then all my workers will wait at once without needing any extra state management.

That's actually a very common mistake when first using tokio::sync::Barrier. While it does create a synchronization point where threads or async tasks can coordinate, you often forget that it only works if all the parties involved call wait() before they proceed. If even one worker bypasses the barrier or tries to access shared data before everyone is ready, your parallel download and process logic will break.

Oh right, I was assuming that as long as I instantiate the barrier once at startup, it would automatically handle the synchronization for every task in my loop without me needing to track which ones have passed.

Exactly. The tokio::sync::Barrier acts like a gatekeeper where every single participant must explicitly call wait(). If you miss calling wait() on one of your download tasks before processing it, that task will proceed prematurely and likely corrupt your shared results or cause data races when they try to write to the same buffer.
I see. So I need to ensure my loop structure guarantees every spawned async task hits that wait() call before attempting to do any actual work on the downloaded items.

You've nailed it. The pattern is usually something like spawning N tasks, passing them all a reference to the same tokio::sync::Barrier instance, and then having each task call await barrier.wait() immediately after starting but before accessing shared resources or writing results back.
That makes sense. But what if I only have 3 workers instead of 10? Does the barrier count change based on how many tasks are actually running at that moment?

Good catch. The tokio::sync::Barrier is created with a specific number of parties from the start, so if you initialize it with 10, all 10 must wait. If you only have 3 active workers but initialized the barrier for 10, those three will block forever because they are waiting for 7 phantom participants that never arrive.
So I should match the barrier count exactly to my active worker pool size in tokio::sync::Barrier.

Precisely. And remember, once everyone has called wait() and released the lock, the barrier is consumed and cannot be reused unless you create a new one or wrap it in some kind of reset logic if your application lifecycle requires multiple synchronization cycles.
Got it. One last question - can I combine this with tokio::sync::Semaphore to control both ordering and rate limiting without over-engineering my download pipeline?

That's a great architectural question. Yes, you absolutely can layer them: use the tokio::sync::Barrier to ensure all downloads finish before processing starts, then wrap your processor in a tokio::sync::Semaphore if you need to throttle concurrent writes or enforce ordering constraints on the final results.
Perfect. So my flow would be spawn tasks -> barrier.wait() -> acquire semaphore -> process and release.

Exactly. That flow gives you strict synchronization points where all downloads are complete before any processing begins, while the tokio::sync::Semaphore adds an extra layer of control over how many concurrent writes happen to your output structure.
Thanks, that clarifies the whole picture for me.
You're welcome. Just remember to keep your barrier count aligned with your actual active workers and never skip the wait() call on any participant.
I'm trying to write a batch downloader that fetches items in parallel using tokio, but I need them all finished before processing starts. What is the best way to synchronize these tasks?

For synchronizing multiple async tasks until they all complete, you should use a tokio::sync::Barrier instead of a manual wait loop or a Mutex-based signal. The Barrier implements a trait that lets you spawn N tasks and call await on the same instance to block them until every one calls it.
That sounds useful, but what exactly does the barrier do under the hood when we hit await?

When you create a tokio::sync::Barrier with N slots, it maintains an internal counter. Each task that calls await decrements this counter internally and releases its token. Only when all tokens are consumed does the barrier unblock every waiting coroutine at once.
So if I have 10 downloaders, do I need to wrap each one in a separate spawn block?

No. You create the barrier once with N = 10, then spawn all your download tasks and attach await on that single barrier instance to each task. They will run concurrently but wait for each other at the barrier point.
Got it. But what happens if a task panics or takes forever? Will others still get unblocked?

If any of the N tasks fails before calling await, that slot never gets consumed and the barrier will remain blocked indefinitely unless you manually release slots. For production code, you must ensure every task calls await or handle errors so they don't leak tokens.
What about cancellation? If I cancel the whole program while some tasks are waiting at the barrier?

If you cancel the runtime before all tasks call await, those tasks will never reach the barrier and their tokens stay held. You should either ensure cancellation happens only after all work is done or use a different synchronization primitive like tokio::sync::Semaphore for optional early exits.
Okay, so the performance benefit? Why not just use threads?

Tokio's barrier is designed to work purely with async runtimes without blocking the event loop. Threads can cause context switches and CPU overhead when waiting, while a tokio::sync::Barrier blocks at the coroutine level using lightweight scheduling.
That makes sense. Can I reuse this pattern if my number of workers changes dynamically?
Yes. You create the barrier with N = expected count, then spawn tasks up to that limit. If you need more than planned, just add extra awaits or increase N when rebuilding the system.
Alright, I think I understand now. Let me sketch this out: create a barrier with 10 slots, spawn 10 async download tasks each awaiting on it, then process results once all finish.
Exactly. That's the core pattern. The barrier ensures strict ordering without manual polling or race conditions, and it scales well with many concurrent tasks in a tokio runtime.

I have a script where I'm downloading multiple files in parallel and then processing them sequentially. My current approach uses a manual loop with a mutex to wait for all downloads before starting the next one, but it feels clunky. Is there a more idiomatic way using tokio's async primitives?

You're right that a manual mutex loop isn't idiomatic for this pattern. The standard solution is to use the tokio::sync::Barrier from the tokio crate, which lets you synchronize multiple tasks until they all arrive at a common point.
I've seen it mentioned, but I'm not sure how exactly I'd integrate it into my download loop. Does this replace the manual wait logic entirely?

Yes, it replaces the manual synchronization completely. You create a Barrier with the number of workers you expect, spawn all your download futures, and then have them all await on that single Barrier before moving to processing.
That sounds cleaner, but I'm worried about error handling. If one download fails, does the barrier block forever or how do we propagate errors?

The Barrier itself doesn't handle errors; it's just a synchronization point. You need to collect results from all futures first, then check if any failed before proceeding. If you use the async barrier pattern correctly with proper error propagation in your loop, you can still let the remaining tasks complete or abort depending on your strategy.

Ah, so I should collect all download futures into a Vec<Future>, then filter out errors before hitting the Barrier. But what if I want to fail fast and stop everything immediately upon the first error?

For that behavior, you wouldn't use the simple barrier pattern alone. Instead, you'd collect all futures into a Vec<Future>, then run them concurrently with tokio::spawn or similar, and inspect their results immediately after each spawn to check for errors before spawning the next one.
Wait, that defeats the purpose of parallelism. If I'm checking one by one, why am I using async primitives? Can we do both?

You can absolutely do both. You spawn all downloads concurrently first without checking errors yet, then use the tokio::sync::Barrier to wait for completion. After everyone hits the barrier, you inspect results and either proceed or abort based on whether any failed.
So the flow is: spawn all -> await barrier -> check errors -> decide next step? And what about the processing phase?

Exactly. After the barrier, you have a Vec of results with any failures marked. You can then either process only successful ones or abort entirely. For the processing phase itself, if it's truly sequential and stateful, you might need another synchronization primitive like tokio::sync::Mutex to protect shared mutable state as you iterate through the filtered results.
I see. So the barrier handles the download group synchronization, but for the actual processing logic where we update counters or write files, I still need a mutex? Is that redundant?

Not necessarily redundant if your processing tasks share mutable state like a running counter or a results vector. The barrier ensures all downloads finish before any processing begins, but the processing itself might involve concurrent writes to shared data structures which still requires protection via tokio::sync::Mutex.

Okay, that clarifies it. So the pattern is: collect futures -> spawn all -> await barrier -> filter/sort results -> process with mutex if needed. Is there anything else I should consider for robustness?

Yes, consider using tokio::select! or similar control flow to handle timeouts on individual downloads within the barrier context, and ensure your error handling propagates cleanly so you can log which specific files failed before deciding to retry or abort.
That's a solid approach. Thanks for walking through that. One last thing - what happens if I have more than 10 workers? Does the barrier scale well?

The Barrier scales fine regardless of worker count since it just waits until N tasks arrive. The main bottleneck will be your actual download speed, not the synchronization overhead, so for most realistic workloads this pattern performs very well.
I'm trying to parallelize a download-and-process pipeline where I need N threads to finish before the next batch starts. Can tokio::sync::Barrier do this?

You are likely thinking of tokio::sync::Barrier, but for async concurrency you should use tokio::sync::mpsc channel or a custom token-based synchronization pattern. A Barrier is useful when all tasks must reach the same point in code together.
So I just wrap my download function in a barrier and pass it to each task?

No, that won't work because tokio::sync::Barrier only works with blocking threads, not async tasks. The Barrier's wait() method blocks the thread until all registered participants arrive. In an async context you need something like a token bucket or a channel-based handshake.
I thought async was just async/await over threads, so maybe I can use Barrier with spawn?

That's a common misconception. Async is not just async/await over threads; it uses cooperative scheduling and event loops where threads don't block each other unless explicitly done so. The tokio runtime handles thread pools internally, but your spawn() calls do not behave like traditional OS threads.
Can I implement my own barrier using channels instead?
Yes, you can use tokio::sync::mpsc channel to coordinate N tasks. Each task sends a token when it finishes its download and receives tokens until all are received.
What about using a counter variable protected by a mutex?
You could use tokio::sync::Mutex with a counter, but that adds unnecessary serialization overhead. Channels are lighter and more idiomatic for async coordination.
So I should avoid mutexes whenever possible in async code?
Generally yes, because tokio::sync::Mutex still requires a thread to lock/unlock. For pure async coordination without blocking threads, channels or atomic counters are better.
Can you give me an example of the channel-based barrier?
You can create a tokio::sync::mpsc Sender and Receiver for N tokens. Each task sends one token when done, and a coordinator collects them until count equals N.
Wait, doesn't that mean I need to manually track the count?
Exactly. You must maintain state outside of individual tasks because async tasks don't share memory directly without synchronization primitives like tokio::sync::Mutex.
What if I use a shared atomic counter instead?

std::sync::AtomicUsize works well with async code since it's lock-free and doesn't require tokio::sync::Mutex. But be aware that std::sync::AtomicUsize is not truly atomic across all platforms without proper synchronization.
I'm getting confused about what actually blocks vs cooperates in async.
Think of it like a round-robin dining table: tasks take turns eating without blocking others unless they explicitly hold a fork (mutex). Channels let you pass signals without holding locks.
So I should use channels for coordination and avoid mutexes unless absolutely necessary?
Yes, that's the general rule. Use tokio::sync::Mutex only when you truly need to serialize access to shared mutable state in a blocking way.
